name: Bedroom Video Access
decision_axis: This lever controls bedroom video access by choosing between public sharing, restricted access for study team only, and complete data suppression.
consequences: Direct effect: Bedroom video access directly affects participant privacy. Downstream implication: Limiting or suppressing bedroom video access may reduce the quality of event-triage tools developed in Aim 3.
options: Publicly share de-identified bedroom videos for external validation and collaboration
Restrict access to study team members only, balancing data sharing with participant privacy concerns
Suppress all bedroom video data due to severe privacy constraints
review: Bedroom Video Access lacks consideration for potential participant re-identification risks in shared videos, even if de-identified. This oversight might compromise participant trust and recruitment.

name: Sensor Tiering
decision_axis: This lever controls sensor tiering by choosing between nightly low-burden monitoring only, scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage, and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events.
consequences: Direct effect: Sensor tiering affects data quality and participant comfort. Downstream implication: Over-reliance on low-burden monitoring might lead to under-captured parasomnia events, compromising Aim 2 characterization.
options: Focus on nightly low-burden monitoring for efficient data collection
Implement scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage to capture critical events
Deploy full PSG after technician-flagged events to ensure clinical-grade resolution
review: Sensor tiering overlooks potential trade-offs between participant comfort and parasomnia event detection, particularly in the escalation nights strategy.

name: Participant Compensation
decision_axis: This lever controls participant compensation by choosing between per-night compensation only, meal provision only, or both for enhanced recruitment.
consequences: Direct effect: Participant compensation affects recruitment and retention. Downstream implication: Insufficient compensation might lead to participant dissatisfaction and reduced data quality.
options: Offer per-night compensation of €80 as the sole incentive
Provide meals in addition to per-night compensation for enhanced participant comfort
Implement both per-night compensation and meal provision for optimal recruitment
review: Participant compensation fails to consider potential biases in participant selection based on socioeconomic factors, potentially skewing study outcomes.

name: Event-Yield Planning
decision_axis: This lever controls event-yield planning by choosing between enrollment targets of 50–70 participants only, extended stays for zero-captured events, or both for optimal data collection.
consequences: Direct effect: Event-yield planning affects participant management and data quality. Downstream implication: Unrealistic enrollment targets might compromise Aim 2 characterization and delay progress toward Aim 3 event-triage tool development.
options: Enroll 50–70 participants as the sole target, with stays capped at 10 weeks
Extend stays for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, up to a maximum of 10 weeks
Implement both enrollment targets and extended stay policies for optimal data collection
review: Event-yield planning overlooks potential participant fatigue and decreased motivation during extended stays, potentially affecting data quality.

name: Recruitment Channels
decision_axis: This lever controls recruitment channels by choosing between the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic only, regional neurologist referrals only, or a combination of both for enhanced participant diversity.
consequences: Direct effect: Recruitment channels affect participant selection and demographics. Downstream implication: Over-reliance on a single recruitment channel might lead to biased participant selection and compromised study outcomes.
options: Recruit exclusively from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic
Use regional neurologist referrals as the sole recruitment strategy
Combine both the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional neurologist referrals for enhanced participant diversity
review: Recruitment channels fail to consider potential biases in participant selection based on socioeconomic factors, potentially skewing study outcomes.

name: Staffing Ratios
decision_axis: This lever controls staffing ratios by choosing between safe coverage only, adequate margin for event response and annotation, or both for optimal participant safety.
consequences: Direct effect: Staffing ratios affect participant safety and data quality. Downstream implication: Insufficient staffing might compromise Aim 1 operational validation and delay progress toward Aim 3 event-triage tool development.
options: Prioritize safe coverage of the 8 active suites only
Ensure adequate margin for event response and annotation, balancing staff availability with participant safety concerns
Implement both safe coverage and adequate margin policies for optimal participant safety
review: Staffing ratios overlook potential trade-offs between staffing costs and Aim 1 operational validation, potentially affecting project timelines.

name: Data Sharing
decision_axis: This lever controls data sharing by choosing between public sharing of de-identified physiological data only, restricted access for study team members only, or complete data suppression due to privacy constraints.
consequences: Direct effect: Data sharing affects participant privacy and collaboration. Downstream implication: Limiting or suppressing data sharing might compromise Aim 3 event-triage tool development and publication.
options: Publicly share de-identified physiological data for external validation and collaboration
Restrict access to study team members only, balancing data sharing with participant privacy concerns
Suppress all data due to severe privacy constraints
review: Data sharing fails to consider potential biases in data selection based on participant demographics, potentially skewing study outcomes.

name: Pharmaceutical Industry Partnership
decision_axis: This lever controls pharmaceutical industry partnership by choosing between no partnership, optional upside only, or mandatory funding requirement for Aim 3 event-triage tool development.
consequences: Direct effect: Pharmaceutical industry partnership affects project funding and collaboration. Downstream implication: Mandatory funding might compromise academic integrity and publication rights.
options: Maintain a neutral stance with no pharmaceutical industry involvement
Engage in an optional partnership for potential upside, retaining full publication rights
Require mandatory funding from the pharmaceutical industry for Aim 3 event-triage tool development
review: Pharmaceutical industry partnership overlooks potential biases in data interpretation based on funding sources, potentially skewing study outcomes.

name: Sensor Calibration
decision_axis: This lever controls sensor accuracy by choosing between standard calibration, iterative refinement, and data-driven re-calibration.
consequences: Standard calibration ensures consistent baseline readings but may overlook subtle changes in participant physiology. Iterative refinement allows for adjustments based on real-time observations but risks introducing bias. Data-driven re-calibration uses machine learning to adapt sensor settings to individual participants' unique characteristics, potentially improving overall accuracy but also increasing complexity.
options: Adhere to standardized manufacturer instructions
Iteratively refine calibration based on technician feedback
Utilize data-driven algorithms to adapt sensor settings
review: Sensor calibration protocols implicitly assume a stable participant population; however, participants with underlying medical conditions (e.g., epilepsy) may exhibit variable physiological responses, necessitating more sophisticated adaptation strategies.

name: Participant Retention
decision_axis: This lever controls participant satisfaction by choosing between personalized support, flexible scheduling, and comprehensive compensation packages.
consequences: Personalized support fosters a sense of belonging among participants but may not be feasible with limited staff resources. Flexible scheduling accommodates participants' varied schedules but risks compromising data quality. Comprehensive compensation packages incentivize participation but could be perceived as coercion.
options: Tailor support to individual participant needs
Offer flexible scheduling options
Provide comprehensive compensation packages
review: Participant retention strategies overlook the potential impact of participant fatigue on data quality; extended stays may lead to decreased motivation and reduced cooperation.

name: Data Visualization
decision_axis: This lever controls data presentation by choosing between detailed technical reports, interactive visualizations, and simplified summary statistics.
consequences: Detailed technical reports facilitate in-depth analysis but may be inaccessible to non-experts. Interactive visualizations engage stakeholders but risk oversimplifying complex findings. Simplified summary statistics provide a high-level overview but may omit crucial context.
options: Produce detailed technical reports for peer-reviewed journals
Develop interactive visualizations for stakeholder engagement
Provide simplified summary statistics for public dissemination
review: Data visualization strategies assume a uniform audience; however, different stakeholders (e.g., policymakers, researchers) require tailored presentations to effectively communicate findings.

name: Staffing Augmentation
decision_axis: This lever controls staff capacity by choosing between hiring additional technicians, outsourcing data analysis, and leveraging existing departmental resources.
consequences: Hiring additional technicians increases staff capacity but may lead to redundancy. Outsourcing data analysis reduces internal workload but could compromise data security. Leveraging existing departmental resources optimizes resource allocation but may introduce communication challenges.
options: Recruit additional research technicians
Outsource data analysis to external vendors
Leverage existing departmental resources
review: Staffing augmentation strategies overlook the potential impact on team dynamics; increased staff numbers could lead to conflicts and decreased morale.

name: Pharmaceutical Partnership
decision_axis: This lever controls industry involvement by choosing between collaborative research, sponsored funding, and exclusive data access.
consequences: Collaborative research fosters a sense of shared ownership but may compromise academic freedom. Sponsored funding provides financial support but could introduce bias. Exclusive data access ensures proprietary control but may limit scientific progress.
options: Engage in collaborative research with pharmaceutical partners
Accept sponsored funding from pharmaceutical companies
Negotiate exclusive data access agreements
review: Pharmaceutical partnership strategies assume a uniform industry response; however, different pharmaceutical companies have varying interests and expectations.
